paper_evidence.summary: KRAS mutations at codons 12/13 (22 of 28 KRAS mutations) were associated with significantly shorter overall and progression-free survival compared to patients without mutations. KRAS mutations were also associated with higher proliferation (PCLI >1%) and TP53 mutations.
paper_evidence.quantitative_data: 22/28 KRAS mutations at codons 12/13; 47% increased hazard of death (p=0.045)

alignment.notes: AI and GT both accurately capture the prognostic significance of KRAS G12/G13 mutations. GT has some redundancy in entries.

paper_evidence.summary: NRAS mutations predominantly occurred at codon 61 (64/74 NRAS mutations) and were associated with aggressive disease features but did not independently affect survival. Features included higher plasma cell percentage, elevated B2M, advanced ISS stage, and more lytic lesions.

alignment.notes: AI correctly identified NRAS Q61 as a diagnostic marker not associated with survival; this evidence is missing from GT

ai_improvements_over_gt.paper_support: 64/74 NRAS mutations at codon 61, associated with aggressive disease features but not survival
RAS mutations associated with malignant transformation from MGUS to MM (7% vs 25% vs 45% in MGUS/new MM/relapsed MM)

overall_judgement.summary: The AI extraction showed high accuracy in capturing the key molecular findings regarding RAS mutations in multiple myeloma, including important distinctions between KRAS and NRAS mutations' prognostic significance. The AI successfully identified several evidence items supported by the paper but missing from the ground truth, particularly regarding NRAS Q61 mutations and the role of RAS mutations in disease progression.
strengths: Accurate capture of differential prognostic impact between KRAS and NRAS mutations
Detailed extraction of mutation-specific information (e.g., codon distribution)
Recognition of both diagnostic and prognostic implications
Identification of important evidence missing from ground truth
weaknesses: Some redundancy in evidence items covering similar findings
Could have more clearly separated primary vs secondary findings
